She Came in Through The Bathroom Window
What Is And What Will Never Be

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well hello there. TV on the radio here. Thomas the Vance. And welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight it's Flashback 1969 and we have archive sessions recorded in that year by Free, by Deep Purple, by Pink Floyd and by Yes.
And also a load of other good music. So do stay tuned.
And just looking back on 1969, it was the year when Paul Wedlinda and John married Yoko,
Midnight Cowboy and Easy Rider were released,
Neil Armstrong made it to the moon, the Woodstock Festival occurred,
America lost its 6,000th plane over Vietnam,
and a lady by the name of Judy Garland died of a drug overdose.
And a man died while the Rolling Stones played at Altamont.
And this is what they were playing.
You fast-talking cats would do if you could. You know I'm ready for the final surprise. There ain't no way around it, ain't nothin' to say. It's gonna satisfy my soul deep inside.
All the prayers and serpents keep the whole place uptight
While it keeps on getting darker outside
I'm a space cowboy
Bet you weren't ready for that
I'm a space cowboy
I'm sure you heard that, yeah, yeah, yeah
I'm a space cowboy
[Music]
Bet you weren't ready for the space
I'm sure you heard that, yeah, yeah, yeah
Steve Miliband from their album called Brave New World.
The track was Space Cowboy.
And that was a guy who was raised in the state of Texas in America
and actually taught the blues by T-Bone Walker
when he was about six years of age and even less.
And before that, a classic track from the Rolling Stones
from an album that came out in December 1969.
The album was called Let It Bleed.
And the title of the track we played, he was Gimme Shelter.
And I played that especially for everybody who likes the Rolling Stones,
particularly William Stephen of Avon
and also Robert Lloyd of Carmarthen,
a lady by the name of Nancy Darby
who lives in Acaster, Malbys, which is near York,
and also a bunch of people called Justin and Liz and Friends
who are at a Gorton house in Tiverton down there in Devon.
And here now is an archive session from 69 by Free.
[Music]
OK, Brian, this number's...
The next number we're going to do is a single released in Great Britain at the moment.
Yeah.
It's one of our own compositions in which Andy plays rhythm guitar,
I play bass and sing, and everybody sings in general.
Yeah.
This is called Broad Daylight.
And that's a band who were given their name by Alexis Corner,
a band by the name of Free in a session they recorded in the BBC studios in 1969.
Paul Rogers on vocal, Paul Kossoff on guitar,
Addie Fraser on bass, and Simon Kirk on the drums.
Paul Kossoff, of course, is no longer with us,
but if you go up to Golders Green Cemetery
and you look at the little commemorative plaque
that relates to his life,
it just says, very simply,
All right, now.
It's like old saxophone joke
And that really is how it fades on the record.
It must be the shortest track he ever recorded.
Bobby Dylan from the album Nashville Skyline
and the song called Country Pie.
And before that, something from a band
who made their name in front of,
according to the reference book,
650,000 people
at a free festival that was held
on July the 5th in 1969.
at Hyde Park.
The band were called King Crimson.
It was a track from their first ever LP.
The album was called In the Court of the Crimson King
and the track was entitled 21st Century Schizoid Man.
And it really is still way out to use an ancient 1969 phrase
in this day and age, but always interesting to listen to.
Requested by loads of people, including Margaret Bell
of Whitley Bay in Tyne and Wear,
Rich George and everybody at the Pocklington School
up there in North Humberside.
I think it's, I can't read the names, I'll just read the address.
everybody at the bungalow in Buck's Farm in Cookeley, which is in Suffolk,
and also by Kevin Andrews, who lives in Worcester,
and by Philip Dunn and all his mates up there in Ayr,
and finally for Paul Armstrong-Rhodes, who lives in Lancaster.
And here's another real classic.
A lineup of Genesis, Gabriel Banks, Anthony Phillips, Mike Rutherford and John Mayhew on the drums
[Music]
[Music]
[Music]
and a track from the first ever Genesis album that came out in 69.
It was called From Genesis to Revelation.
The track was entitled In the Beginning.
And they were discovered in that year by Jonathan King,
who had actually gone to the same school as them,
that school being Charterhouse.
But that album came out on the Decca label.
It got absolutely no attention whatsoever.
Nobody wanted to know.
And it was a year, I think, before they recorded anything else
because Jonathan King dropped them, and so indeed did Decca.
But look at them now.
Prior to that, you heard David Bowie from the album David Bowie.
and a thing that's been a hit single twice, Space Oddity.
And that piece of music really marked the change in his career
because prior to that he was recording things like The Laughing Gnome.
Do you remember that? Weird little gimmicky record.
A few letters here.
Francis Wolfe of Appleby in Cumbria is a happy person at the moment.
So indeed I hope are Jim and Robert and Stuart and Gary and Craig
and James 1 and James 2 and Peter.
Best wishes to all of you up there in East Kilbride.
Letter here from Gordon who wants me to say happy birthday to Jacqueline
who is 19 tomorrow, also mentioned Tommy and Tony and Elaine.
And we played that David Bowie track for Biffo Smith,
what a good name, of Alverston in Derby.
Now, the original line-up of Deep Purple were Blackmore, Lorde, Pace,
Nick Simper and Rod Evans, and here they are.
[Music]
and you heard the painter, Lelenia and M. Areta.
The time now is 11 minutes to 11 o'clock.
You're listening to the Friday Rock Show and Flashback 69.
And we played that Deep Purple set for a load of people, actually.
That's Dave and Mark.
I think it's Mark and Tim and Adam and Miss Davis and Jeremy Morris,
who all sort of convened in the sixth form at Tring School.
And also for Caroline Halstead, who lives in Woodworth in Lancashire.
No introduction necessary for this mob.
The man who plays the most abrasive guitar in the world.
[Music]
That's Neil Young, along with a band called Crazy Horse,
who used to be called The Rockets before he renamed them,
and a track from the album called Everybody Knows This Is Nowhere.
The album came out in January 1969.
The track we played was Cinnamon Girl.
Sorry about the jump, but then the album is 11 years old
and it has been played a bit.
Played it for Dave Robbins of Crew in Cheshire.
Before that, something that was chronologically
from the last tracks the Beatles ever, ever cut together
from the album Abbey Road.
Three cuts, me, Mr Mustard, Polythene Pam,
and she came in through the bathroom window.
And that was for all the hard-working and underpaid people
at Langley Park School, particularly Peter Biggles Atkinson,
the pound you sent us, Peter.
I think I'll put it in the poor box round the local pub.
Seriously, I won't drink it, I'll bung it in the poor box.
And we also played that Beatles set for Steve,
the block of wit withy, I think it is,
in Bristol.
And here's something strange but lovely.
B-U-T-S-R-Q-B-O-N-M-O-K-J-I-H-G-F-E-D-C-B-A
The album that produced the anthem,
[Music]
[Music]
Whole Lotta Love, Led Zeppelin II.
That was Led Zeppelin and What Is
and What Should Never Be.
And before that, you heard something from
Soft Machine, the first album by the band
who convened in and around Canterbury.
And the track that we played was called
A Concise British Alphabet, Part 2.
The actual ingredients were Hello Dare, and Dada was here.
And we played that set for Mark Bishop,
who lives in Redcar in Cleveland, and all his mates,
and also for Bev and Rastus and Snapper and Ritchie
and Arnie in Winchester,
and for Geoffrey Hook and all his friends in Bingley,
and for Ian Stephen, who lives in Loughborough,
and for Tony, who lives in [redacted address], Walls End,
in Tyne and Weir.
And now here's the original line-up of Yes,
and the first ever session they ever did
for the BBC in 1969.
The very first session that Yes ever recorded for the BBC,
[Music]
Something's Coming and Sweetness,
which incidentally comprised their first ever single
that came out in June 1969.
The original line-up, John Anderson, Chris Squire,
Peter Banks, Tony Kaye and Bill Bruford.
And that was for Yes fans all around the country.
And a lot of people have written in asking me
to try and find out what Yes are doing at the moment.
And now I'll read you something that I got in my hand
an hour and a half ago.
In a stunning and unexpected move,
yes members chris squire steve howe and alan white have enlisted multi-keyboardist jeff downs and
vocalist trevor horn to replace john anderson and rick wakeman horn and downs have recently won
worldwide acclaim with their album living in the plastic age and performing under the name of the
buggles founding yes member chris squire states we are a revitalized and regenerated band working
together once more as a strong unified group we are playing more positively and powerfully than
ever before and we feel that now is the time we're going to reinvent the yes idea yes has rejoined
with producer eddie offered and word has it that roger dean is going to be back developing the
artwork for the upcoming album and the tour program brian lane states that the show uh their
european program which is going to be in october early november will not be affected at all they
will be doing yes classics the entire new album which is presently being recorded and a few
surprises. John Anderson will continue doing his
solo projects and working with other people.
Rick Wakeman has formed his own band
to be called the Rick Wakeman Band
and they're going to be touring Europe soon.
The album, when it comes out by
the newly put together Yes, will be called
Machine Messiah and it'll be out
in mid-July. A few jokes
are kicking around at the moment. Some people are
saying they ought to call themselves the Yuggles
or maybe just maybe. I
personally think it's a great idea and I wish
them well.
The everybody, everywhere is in the same.
That way I can know who won the war.
...as they performed at Woodstock from the album called Woodstock One.
The track was We're Not Gonna Take It.
And if you wonder why they're so out of tune, I'll tell you why.
John Entwistle said on the air last year
that when they actually did their performance at Woodstock,
they were completely wiped out
because somebody spiked the drinks with LSD
they had just before they went on stage.
And before that, you heard Crosby, Stills, Nash and Young
and Wooden Ships, also, of course, from the album Woodstock.
And a gentleman by the name of James Bechira wanted to hear that
and also his name on the air.
Better known by Santana, but that's the original version being sung there by the man who wrote it,
[Music]
[Music]
[Music]
Peter Green, along with Fleetwood Mac from the 69 album The Pious Bird of Good Omen.
The track, of course, was Black Magic Woman.
And before that, something from, I think,
the best album Jethro Tull ever made,
the album called Stand Up.
It was their second LP,
and the track was called We Used To Know.
Rumour has it, unconfirmed rumour has it,
that Jethro Tull are going through major changes at the moment.
We played that set for Paul Locum
and all his friends over there in New Zealand,
who, through the miracle of various things,
are going to be hearing this programme,
and also for everybody who goes to the Sutton High School
for Boys in Plymouth,
and for Barbara and Sudi Patel,
who live in Killern in Stirlingshire as well.
We'd have to play something by the Floyd, and we're going to.
Here they are, in session, from 1969.
The Pink Floyd from a session they put together in 1969.
You heard Green is the Colour and The Narrow Way.
We played that for Rosemary,
who's a lady with exceedingly good taste in music, I'm told,
by a gentleman by the name of Steve, who lives in Rochford.
And also I played it for Nick Hepp.
Actually, Heripath, I think the name is,
for his brother who's having a birthday on Monday
and also for a gentleman who does really, really good artwork,
a gentleman by the name of Philip England and Floyd Fanatics.
On July the 25th, we're going to rebroadcast
that epic thing called The War
with commentary by Roger Waters.
And we finish with Cream.
I grow glad, I'm glad, I'm so glad, I'm glad, I'm glad.
[Music]
and the track called I'm So Glad.
for Simon Baggett of Sheffield
and all cream lovers everywhere.
Message here to Ian of Manchester.
Angela sends love to you.
She's in Loughborough
and she'll see you at the station tomorrow.
And that's it for Flashback 69.
Hope you enjoyed the programme.
Wallowing in sort of nostalgia,
which is a pleasant thing to do
every once in a while.
Next week, we return to the music of the 80s
with a repeat of the session by Angel Witch
and the first ever session
by the band called More.
Our engineers tonight were Peter and Jonathan.
Peter and Jonathan,
to give him his correct title.
thank you. Mr Wilson, underneath that
caftan, what do you have to say? Don't miss
more next week, they're good. They're good
he says they're good, he ought to know, he recorded them
Rock on Saturday tomorrow, national previews of
Graham Parker's new album and the one by David
Coverdale, which is right good as well
I'm Tommy Vance in London, wherever you are
take care of yourselves, goodnight
and God bless
It's midnight and we're together on all wavelengths
275 and VHF
and this is Tim Gudgeon in the Midnight Newsroom
The headlines, inflation is
now almost 22 percent.
